coredata.dc:title: Lignin based carbon fiber fabrics with hybrid doping approach as self-standing electrodes for supercapacitors
coredata.dc:creator: Supchocksoonthorn, Preeyanuch
Hrimchum, Nathakorn
Budsrirak, Thanyarak
Intaraprasit, Sunyalux
Thongsai, Nichaphat
Aussawasathien, Darunee
coredata.dc:description: Novel carbon fiber (CF) mats were fabricated from tobacco leaf-based carbon dots (TL-CDs) filled rubber wood chip-extracted lignin (RWC-L)/polyacrylonitrile (PAN) composite fibers with a hybrid doping method. The ex-situ doping was applied during fiber formation via electrospinning by adding functionalized TL-CDs in the spinning solution. The in-situ doping was carried out in carbonization process for nitrogen (N) and oxygen (O) atom generation by utilizing RWC-L and PAN as a mixed precursor. The CF properties and electrochemical performances were evidently influenced by TL-CD content and carbonization temperature. The CF fabric containing 0.4% w/v of TL-CDs and carbonized at 1200 °C provided optimal specific capacitance (Cs ≅ 225 F g−1), energy density (ED ≅ 53 W h kg−1), and power density (PD ≅ 130 W kg−1) at a current density of 0.2 A g−1 in 6 M KOH electrolyte. It also had excellent cycling stability of ≅ 90% after 12,000 cycles at a current density of 5 A g−1. This could be combined effects of a small fiber diameter (≅ 0.65 μm), a high heteroatom content (≅ 21 wt% of N and O atoms), a large surface area (≅ 1063 m2 g−1), a high micropore volume (≅ 2.41 cm3 g−1), high graphitic carbon (ID/IG ratio ≅ 0.97), and high electrical conductivity (≅ 62 S cm−1). Moreover, the electrochemical properties of the TL-CD added CF sheet were superior to those of the bare CF mat. Therefore, this work introduces a sustainable and efficient approach to develop lignin-based CF fabrics as self-standing electrodes for advanced energy-storage devices.

SEM images and fiber-diameter-distribution graphs of electro-spun composite fibers at different TL-CD concentrations: (a, e) 0.0CD-fibers, (b, f) 0.2CD-fibers, (c, g) 0.4CD-fibers, and (d, h) 0.6CD-fibers.

SEM micrographs and fiber-diameter-distribution graphs of CFs at different TL-CD loadings and carbonization temperatures: (a) 0.0CD-C1000, (b) 0.2CD-C1000, (c) 0.4CD-C1000, (d) 0.6CD-CD1000, (e) 0.4CD-C900, (f) 0.4CD-C1200, and (g) 0.4CD-C1400. Insets are high-magnification SEM images of each condition.
(a) TEM image of 0.4CD-C1200 and (b) size distribution histograms of TL-CDs in the fiber structure.
(a) N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms at 77k and (b) pore size distribution (BJH method) of CFs prepared using various TL-CD dosages and carbonization temperatures.

(a) Survey XPS spectra of 0.0CD-C1200 and 0.4CD-C1200, high-resolution spectra of 0.0CD-C1200: (b) C1s, (c) O1s, and (d) N1s regions, and deconvoluted curves of 0.4CD-C1200: (e) C1s, (f) O1s, and (g) N1s areas.
(a) overlayed XRD patterns and (b) overlayed Raman spectra after curve-fitting of TL-CD/RWC-L/PAN CFs at various CD contents and carbonization temperatures.

CV curves of CF electrodes at different scan rates in 6 M KOH electrolyte: (a) 0.0CD-C1000, (b) 0.2CD-C1000, (c) 0.4CD-C1000, and (d) 0.6CD-C1000. (e) overlayed CV curves of CF electrodes with varied TL-CD contents at a scan rate of 5 mV s−1 using 6 M KOH electrolyte.

CV curves of 0.4%w/v CD filled CF electrodes at different temperatures using various scan rates in 6 M KOH electrolyte: (a) 0.4CD-C900, (b) 0.4CD-C1200, and (c) 0.4CD-C1400. (d) overlayed CV curves of 0.4%w/v CD filled CF electrodes carbonized at different temperatures using a scan rate of 5 mV s−1 and 6 M KOH electrolyte.

GCD cycles of CF electrodes containing 0.4% w/v of TL-CDs in 6 M KOH electrolyte: (a) at different carbonization temperatures using a current density of 0.2 A g−1, (b) at various current densities using the 0.4CD-C1200 electrode. (c) plots of Cs values versus current densities of 0.4% w/v of TL-CD doped CF electrodes at varied carbonization temperatures using 6 M KOH electrolyte.

(a) Ragone plot of the 0.4CD-C1200 electrode at different current densities in 6 M KOH electrolyte (six dots on the line), (b) Nyquist plot of the 0.0CD-C1000 and 0.4CD-C1200 electrodes (inset shows a zoomed plot), and (c) CR at various charge-discharge cycles of the 0.4CD-C1200 electrode using a Sawagelok cell in 6 M KOH electrolyte (inset depicts GCD curves at 1st and 12000th cycles).

head.ce:title: Lignin based carbon fiber fabrics with hybrid doping approach as self-standing electrodes for supercapacitors

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Novel carbon fiber (CF) mats were fabricated from tobacco leaf-based carbon dots (TL-CDs) filled rubber wood chip-extracted lignin (RWC-L)/polyacrylonitrile (PAN) composite fibers with a hybrid doping method. The ex-situ doping was applied during fiber formation via electrospinning by adding functionalized TL-CDs in the spinning solution. The in-situ doping was carried out in carbonization process for nitrogen (N) and oxygen (O) atom generation by utilizing RWC-L and PAN as a mixed precursor. The CF properties and electrochemical performances were evidently influenced by TL-CD content and carbonization temperature. The CF fabric containing 0.4% w/v of TL-CDs and carbonized at 1200 °C provided optimal specific capacitance (Cs ≅ 225 F g−1), energy density (ED ≅ 53 W h kg−1), and power density (PD ≅ 130 W kg−1) at a current density of 0.2 A g−1 in 6 M KOH electrolyte. It also had excellent cycling stability of ≅ 90% after 12,000 cycles at a current density of 5 A g−1. This could be combined effects of a small fiber diameter (≅ 0.65 μm), a high heteroatom content (≅ 21 wt% of N and O atoms), a large surface area (≅ 1063 m2 g−1), a high micropore volume (≅ 2.41 cm3 g−1), high graphitic carbon (ID/IG ratio ≅ 0.97), and high electrical conductivity (≅ 62 S cm−1). Moreover, the electrochemical properties of the TL-CD added CF sheet were superior to those of the bare CF mat. Therefore, this work introduces a sustainable and efficient approach to develop lignin-based CF fabrics as self-standing electrodes for advanced energy-storage devices.

ce:list-item.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Renewable and green energy storage systems, such as batteries, emergency power supplies, and supercapacitors, have been increasingly popular and attracted attention in research and in global markets. Among these technologies, supercapacitors are particularly prominent for their high power density, fast charge/discharge ability, great cycling stability, and environmental friendliness [1,2]. Recently, porous carbon electrodes have become promising in supercapacitors or electrical-double-layer capacitors (EDLCs), which can store electrical energy at the electrode/electrolyte interface. Various carbon forms can be used as electrode materials such as graphene [3], carbon nanotube (CNT) [4], activated carbon (AC) [5], and carbon fiber (CF) [6].

Typically, electrode materials need to possess high specific surface area along with optimized pore characteristics [7], sufficient electrical conductivity [8,9], and excellent electrochemical stability [10] for high performance supercapacitors. To achieve those requirements, active materials and conductive additives in powder form are commonly mixed with a binder to support all components in the electrodes [11,12]. However, most binders such as polytetrafluoroethylene have high electrical resistance, leading to a reduction of electroactive sites on electrode surfaces for ion exchange [13].

Porous CF mats offer a high possibility to be free-standing electrodes without any binder and chemical/physical activation, owing to their nanometer to sub-micron fiber sizes with large specific surface area, light weight, sufficient thermal and electrical conductivities, high strength, and outstanding cycle life [14–16]. Nevertheless, pores in CFs are mainly tortuous micropores with little connectivity, leading to long diffusion paths and partial access for ions to pore volumes. Hence, numerous ways were proposed to modify the pore structure and improve the efficiency of ion accessibility including using different precursors to adjust pore size and volume [17], doping of heteroatoms to increase the wettability and conductivity [18], and utilizing appropriate electrolytes [19].

Polyacrylonitrile (PAN) has been generally used as a precursor for producing CF materials due to its superior strength, stability, and high carbon yield [20]. However, its high cost is one of the limiting issues for large-scale production [21]. In order to develop sustainable and renewable electrode materials, biomass-based lignin has been considered as a precursor to partially or fully replace PAN because it has high carbon content, specific aromatic structure, high strength, and plenty resources [15,22]. Rubber wood chips (RWCs) derived from the chopped rubber wood and furniture factories are generated in large quantity of approximately two million tons annually [23]. The rubber plantation in southern part of Thailand is one of the largest region in the world [23]. In general, RWC consists of 39% cellulose, 29% hemicellulose, and 28% lignin [24]. Several methods such as organosolv, kraft, soda, and sulfite processes are normally utilized to extract lignin from hardwoods, softwoods, and herbaceous plants [25–28]. Among these, an organosolv process can produce lignin with low amount of ash and sulfur element [22]. Lignin based CFs have attracted much attention for use as electrode materials in supercapacitors because many aromatic rings in lignin structure can provide charge transfer at the electrode surface, therefore, enhancing capacitive performances [29]. However, extracted lignin has relatively low molecular weight, not suitable for fiber formation in electrospinning process. Consequently, adding other polymers in the spinning solution is necessary to enhance the solution viscosity for practical spinnability [15,22]. Other limitations of lignin derived CFs as electrodes in supercapacitors are their low wettability and effective surface areas due to the predominant carbon atoms, causing hydrophobicity at the electrode surface [15,16,22]. This drawback can be overcome by adding carbon dots (CDs) into the structure. CDs are classified as optically and electronically unique nanomaterials (average particle dimension ≤ 10 nm). CDs are composed of conjugated carbon cores with many surface functionalities such as hydroxyl, amino, carbonyl, and carboxyl groups [30,31]. These particular structures offer high hydrophilicity, good conductivity, and surface-sensitive properties [32]. Hence, CDs are utilized as additives in electrode materials for supercapacitors and achieve great capacitive performances. For example, Yu et al. [33] presented the use of CDs coated onto lignin-based activated carbon as composite electrodes for supercapacitors. The capacitance was enhanced from 125.8 to 301.7 F g−1 with the presence of CDs.

To the best of our knowledge, there is no report on using both in-situ and ex-situ doping during CF fabrication from CD filled RWC-L/PAN composite fibers through electrospinning and thermal treatment process. In this work, the ex-situ doping was generated during fiber formation via electrospinning by adding functionalized CDs as additive materials in the spinning solution. The in-situ doping was carried out by utilizing RWC-L and PAN as a mixed precursor with a high fraction of a bio-renewable carbon-based lignin to create nitrogen (N) and oxygen (O) functionalities in carbonization process. Influences of CD concentrations and carbonization temperatures on CF properties and electrochemical performances were also systematically investigated. It was expected that this hybrid doping method could offer low ionic diffusion resistance and great wettability on CF surfaces due to the presence of high heteroatom content, resulting in the improvement of capacitive behavior. Therefore, this work offered a simple, effective, and beneficial approach for producing heteroatom-doped CF sheets as binder-free electrode materials with great capacitive properties. The schematic representation of the overall concept of RWC-L, PAN, and tobacco leaf-based CD (TL-CD) conversion into a self-standing electrode for supercapacitor is displayed in Fig. 1 .

RWC-L with low ash content was obtained from the National Center for Genetic Engineering and Biotechnology (BIOTEC, Thailand) using an organosolv extraction [34, 35]. It possessed an average molecular weight (Mw) of ≅ 1927 g mol−1, a polydispersity (PDI) of ≅ 1.4, and ash content < 0.3%. PAN (Mw = 150,000 g mol−1), dimethylformamide (DMF), potassium hydroxide (KOH), sulfuric acid (H2SO4), and sodium hydroxide (NaOH) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. Tobacco leaves (TLs) were bought from a local supermarket in Thailand for CD synthesis. Air zero and N2 (99.995% purity) gasses from Linde (Thailand) Public Co. Ltd. were used through thermal stabilization and carbonization, respectively. Deionized (DI) water with a resistivity > 18 MΩ cm was used in this work.

Briefly, small pieces of TLs (10 g) and H2SO4 solution (0.5 M, 20 mL) were placed into a crucible and heated at 200 °C for 3 h. After the reaction, the crucible was cooled down to room temperature. The brown solution was filtered, neutralized with 10 M NaOH solution, and then centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 15 min. After that, the CD solution was purified using a dialysis membrane for 72 h and freeze-dried to yield the brown solid product as CDs (0.3530 g, 3.53% yield).

Different concentrations of TL-CDs (0.0, 0.2, 0.4, and 0.6%w/v) were filled in 5 mL of DMF, containing a constant composition of RWC-L (10% w/v) and PAN (6% w/v). This RWC-L to PAN ratio was chosen based on the feasibility of fiber formation and narrow fiber- size-distribution (see supplementary data). The mixed solution was stirred at 100 °C for 1 h. After that, the dark-brown solution was poured in a 1 mL syringe and then electro-spun using a feeding rate of 0.05 mL min−1 and an applied voltage of 16 kV. The tip-to-collector distance and the rotation speed of the collector were maintained at 10 cm and 160 rpm, respectively. Nonwoven fiber mats were named as 0.0CD-fiber, 0.2CD-fiber, 0.4CD-fiber, and 0.6CD-fiber, corresponding to the CD loading.

The fiber mat was thermally stabilized at 250 °C for 1 h with a heating rate of 0.5 °C min−1 and air flow rate of 2 L min−1 in a tube furnace. To study the effect of TL-CD contents, the stabilized fiber fabric was subsequently carbonized at 1000 °C for 1 h under N2 flow rate of 2 L min−1 and a heating rate of 2.5 °C min−1. The obtained CFs were denoted as 0.0CD-C1000, 0.2CD-C1000, 0.4CD-C1000, and 0.6CD-C1000. The RWC-L/PAN fiber fabric containing 0.4%w/v of TL-CD concentration was selected to further study the influence of carbonization temperatures at 900, 1000, 1200, and 1400 °C using same conditions as mentioned before. The carbonized fibers were marked as 0.4CD-C900, 0.4CD-C1000, 0.4CD-C1200, and 0.4CD-C1400, respectively. The electro-spun fiber fabric was placed between two alumina plates under heat treatment to receive a flat sheet.

The surface functional group of TL-CDs was characterized using a Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectrometer (Spectrum 2000, Perkin Elmer). The TL-CDs were mixed with KBr powder prior to making a pellet sample. The FTIR spectrum was recorded between 4000 and 900 cm−1 with a speed of 1 cm s−1, 3 scans, and 1 cm−1 of resolution. The dimension and morphology of TL-CDs were determined using a high-resolution transmission electron microscope (Tecnai G2 F20, FEI Corporation) at 10 kV. The sample was uniformly dispersed in ethanol using an ultrasonic bath. After that, the suspension was dropped on a copper (Cu) grid before TEM operation. The particle size distribution of TL-CD suspension in DI water was measured using a nanoparticle analyzer (Nano Partica SZ-100, Horiba) together with a dynamic-light-scattering technique.

The atomic composition (C, N, and O) on the surface of CDs and CFs (≤ 10 nm in depth) was measured using an X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (XPS) (Kratos Analytical Ltd.) with monochromatic Al Kα (1.487 keV) gun as an X-ray source. The XPS spectra were collected under an emission current of 15 mA and a resolution of 160. The spectra were fitted using an ESCApe program. The elemental composition of the whole CF sample including C, hydrogen (H), N, and O was determined using a combustion CHN analyzer (CHN628, LECO Instruments (Thailand) Ltd.) following ASTM D5373–16.

Surface topologies of CFs were analyzed using a Schottky field-emission scanning electron microscope (FE-SEM, SU500, Coax Group Corp. Ltd.). The fiber sheet was attached on the substrate using a piece of carbon tape and then coated with a thin layer of gold for 60 s before SEM analysis. The fiber diameter distribution was determined using Image J software together with SEM micrographs.

A surface area analyzer (Micromeritics, ASAP2460) was used to characterize the N2 adsorption-desorption isotherm, surface area (Brunauer-Emmett-Teller, (SBET)), and pore characteristics (Barrett-Joyner-Halenda (BJH)) of CFs.

The graphitic aspect of CFs was evaluated using a high-resolution Raman spectrometer (Horiba Jobin Yvon, Model L26Ram HR Evolution) with a 532-nm laser. An x-ray diffractometer (Rigaku, TTRAX III) using CuKα radiation (0.154 nm) was employed to obtain x-ray diffraction (XRD) peaks at a voltage of 40 kV and a current of 30 mA. The scan rate was 5° min−1 with a resolution of 0.02° (2θ).

The electrical resistivity of CFs was determined using a four-point-probe equipment (Jandel Model 3, Jandel Engineering Ltd.) according to ASTEM D4496. As-spun fiber sheets were initially cut into a spherical shape with a diameter of 1 cm and a thickness of 0.4 mm. A pressure of 0.1 ton was applied on ten layers of cut fiber-sheets in a mold to make a pellet-like sample. Subsequently, compressed samples were thermally stabilized and carbonized, following described methods in Section 2.4. The electrical conductivity ( σ ) was calculated using Eq. (1) [16]:

where l , w , t , and R are length, width, thickness, and surface resistivity of the CF sample.

The electrochemical analysis was carried out using an AUTOLAB potentiostat instrument (Metrohm, Autolab, 302 N) at room temperature. The measurement of cyclic voltammetry (CV) and electrochemical impedance spectrometry (EIS) were performed in a three-electrode system. Ag/AgCl and bare platinum (Pt)-mesh holder (99.99% purity, 1.25 cm x 1.25 cm) connected with a Pt wire (99.99% purity and a length of 5 cm) were used as a reference electrode and a counter electrode, respectively. The CF mat with a dimension of 0.5 cm x 0.5 cm x 0.4 mm (≅ 0.003 g) kept inside the other Pt-mesh holder (same structure and dimension as a counter electrode) was used as a working electrode. The distance between each electrode and 30 mL of 6.0 M KOH electrolyte were also fixed throughout the measurement. The set-up apparatus of three electrode system and its components for electrochemical measurement are demonstrated in the supporting data. The CV measurement was done at various scan rates of 5, 10, 20, 30, 50, and 100 mV s−1 and a potential window in the range of −1.2 to 0.1 V. The EIS technique was measured in the frequency range from 100 kHz to 0.1 Hz at an AC amplitude of 0.01 V. Galvanostatic charge-discharge (GCD) analysis was tested in a two-electrode system using a Swagelok cell at different current densities including 0.2, 0.3, 0.5, 1.0, 2.0, and 5.0 A g−1. The specific capacitance (Cs, F g−1) was calculated using Eq. (2) [15].

Typically, CDs can be synthesized from synthetic chemicals and bio-based materials with high carbon content [31-33,36]. For the renewable and sustainable point of view, TL was selected as a raw material for CD synthesis. The obtained TL-CDs were quasi-spherical nanoparticles in crystalline phase, judging from the appearance of 0.21 nm fringes, as shown in Fig. 2a . Moreover, their nanoparticle diameter was in the range of 4–9 nm with an average size of 6.7 nm (Fig. 2b).

The FT-IR spectrum of prepared TL-CDs is depicted in Fig. 3a . A broad vibration band at 3400 cm−1 is assigned to hydroxyl (OH) and amine (NH) groups [37]. The vibration bands at 2932, 1560–1680, and 1053 cm−1 correspond to alkyl (CH), alkenyl/carbonyl (C = C/C = O), and CO stretching, respectively [38]. The vibration band of CH bending is observed at 1409 cm−1 [38].

Fig. 3b-e illustrate the XPS patterns of TL-CDs. The survey XPS spectrum exhibited strong signals of C1s, N1s, and O1s at 285.9, 400.9, and 532.9 eV with atomic concentrations of 65.71, 6.57, and 23.97%, respectively (Fig. 3b). The deconvolution of the C1s peak demonstrated four signals at 284.7, 286.5, 287.8, and 288.8 eV, corresponding to C = C/CC, COH/CN, C = O, and OC = O, respectively (Fig. 3c) [31,39]. High resolution signals of the O1s peak (Fig. 3d) located at 530.9, 531.9, 532.8, and 534.1 eV, indicating C = O, CO-C, COH, and C = O (aromatic), respectively [31,36,40]. The deconvolution of the N1s peak (Fig. 3e) revealed three different types of N elements at 398.5, 399.8, and 401.2 eV, ascribed to pyridinic N, graphitic N, and NH, respectively [41,42]. Both FT-IR and XPS results suggested that the synthesized TL-CDs had high sp2-C existence and O- and N-containing functional groups.

Electrospinning is well-known as a simple and low-cost technology for the production of fiber webs with fiber diameters in nano to micro scales and a large surface area, thus providing outstanding advantages for energy storage devices [43]. As shown in Fig. 4a -h, the as-spun fibers without TL-CD addition possessed fiber diameters in the range of 1.0–3.5 µm, which were quite larger than those of the composite fibers with TL-CD loadings ( ≅ 0.6–1.8 µm). The presence of CDs made spinning solutions more conductive, leading to higher electrical forces to stretch out fibers [44]. The higher the TL-CD content, the narrower the fiber diameter distribution was received. Furthermore, rougher and more porous surfaces of the TL-CD filled composite fibers were obtained in comparison with unfilled fibers due to the accumulation of CD particles on fiber surfaces as well as rapid solvent evaporation [45].

The composite fiber mats with different TL-CD concentrations were subjected to thermal stabilization and carbonization for producing CFs with high carbon content and modified surface chemistry and morphology. The surface morphology and dimensional distribution of CFs at different TL-CD loadings and carbonization temperatures were investigated as shown in Fig. 5a -g. At carbonization temperature of 1000 °C, the fiber diameter of CFs with different TL-CD loadings became smaller (
≅
0.2–1.4 µm), compared with as-spun TL-CD/RWC-L/PAN fibers. This was attributed to the elimination of volatile matters during high temperature treatment. During thermal stabilization, electro-spun fibers undergo cyclization, oxidation, dehydrogenation, and crosslinking to prevent fiber fusion at high temperature in carbonization step [46]. Moreover, a low heating rate is usually set-up to prevent side reactions and achieve complete oxidation process. In carbonization process, the non-carbon elements are almost eliminated along with the formation of graphitic carbon structure [47]. However, the fiber diameter of CFs doped with TL-CDs at various concentrations ( ≅ 0.4–1.4 µm) were slightly larger than that of the 0.0CD-C1000 (
≅
0.2–1.0 µm), since CDs could not be removed at elevated temperature owing to their high heat resistance. The 0.4CD-C1000 sample showed the narrowest fiber diameter distribution in the range of 0.6–1.2 µm, compared with others at varied CD dosages. Therefore, the 0.4CD-C1000 was chosen to study the effect of carbonization temperatures. It was found that the surface roughness with many tiny pores was obviously observed at high temperatures, e.g., 1200 and 1400 °C because more volatile substances could be efficiently eliminated, in good accordance with obtained yields. The yield of CFs containing 0.4%w/v of TL-CDs reduced from 43 to 30% with increasing carbonization temperature from 900 to 1400 °C. The 0.4CD-C1200 had the most homogeneous fiber formation with the narrowest fiber diameter distribution in the range of 0.4–1.0 µm.

The morphology and size of TL-CDs attached on the 0.4CD-C1200 (as an example) were investigated using TEM (Fig. 6a and b). The TEM image exhibited the TL-CD dispersion in the fiber structure with an average size of 8.5 nm, indicating the presence of partial TL-CD agglomeration.

Fig. 7a and b show N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms and pore size distribution, respectively, of CFs at various CD dosages and carbonization temperatures. N2 adsorption-desorption aspects of the CFs displayed type I isotherms according to the IUPAC classification [48], indicative of Langmuir adsorption behavior of microporous materials. The pore-size-distribution graphs exhibited that as-prepared CFs mainly contained micropores along with some mesopores. The 0.2CD-C1000 possessed higher mesopores on its structure than other samples. At 0.2%w/v of CD loading, the dispersion of CD particles might be more homogeneous than those CFs with high CD contents, leading to more uniform thermal conductivity on the fiber structure to create continuous growth of pore cracking [49]. Table 1 shows the surface area and pore characteristics of CFs, relative to the N2 adsorption-desorption and pore size distribution results. The average pore size of prepared CFs was approximately 0.54–1.10 nm together with high proportion of micropores up to 84–99%. The SBET of CFs containing various CD contents at carbonization temperature of 1000 °C were rather similar (712–770 m2 g−1), except for that of the 0.2CD-C1000 (488 m2 g−1) as a result of high portion of mesopores. The 0.4CD-C1000 had the highest micropore volume (Vmicro) of ≅ 1.93 cm3 g−1 and the total pore volume (Vtotal) of ≅ 1.95 cm3 g−1 along with the lowest mesopore volume (Vmeso) of ≅ 0.02 cm3 g−1. In contrast, the 0.2CD-C1000 offered the lowest Vmicro (≅ 0.74 cm3 g−1) and Vtotal (≅ 0.88 cm3 g−1) with the highest Vmeso (≅ 0.14 cm3 g−1). As the carbonization temperature was increased from 900 to 1200 °C, the SBET, Vmicro, and Vtotal of the CFs containing 0.4% w/v of TL-CDs increased greatly from 705 to 1063 m2 g−1, 1.08 to 2.41 cm3 g−1, and 1.10 to 2.44 cm3 g−1, respectively. When the carbonization temperature was raised to 1400 °C, the SBET, Vmicro, and Vtotal of 0.4CD-C1400 significantly decreased because the pore structure might be vastly destroyed and collapsed at high temperature [16]. Furthermore, the 0.4CD-C1200 provided the highest SBET, Vtotal, and Vmicro, which would be beneficial for use as a high capacitive-electrode-material [50].

Fig. 8a -g illustrate the XPS patterns of 0.0CD-C1200 and 0.4CD-C1200 as examples, providing the binding energy (BE) peaks as well as chemical composition on fiber surfaces. The survey spectrum of the 0.0CD-C1200 sample exhibited three intense signals of C1s, N1s, and O1s at 284.6, 401.1, and 532.2 eV with atomic concentrations of 92.50, 1.98, and 5.52%, respectively (Fig. 8a), which were insignificantly different from those of the 0.4CD-C1200 sample due to low CD-doping concentration. The survey spectrum of the 0.4CD-C1200 sample also showed three signals of C1s, N1s, and O1s at 284.6, 401.1, and 532.5 eV, respectively, including atomic concentrations of 92.77 (C), 1.57 (N), and 5.66 (O)% (Fig. 8a). The results corresponded to the previous report that carbonaceous materials from biomasses are mainly rich in N and O functionalities [51]. For both 0.0CD-C1200 and 0.4CD-C1200 samples, the C1s region was deconvoluted into six signals of CC, CH, CO/CN, C = O, O = CO, and C1s (shake-up) (Fig. 8b and e). The high-resolution spectra of O1s revealed four different peaks of OH, CO/CN, absorbed with H2O, and C = O (Fig. 8c and F). Lastly, the high-resolution spectra of N1s showed three signals of NH, CN, and NO (Fig. 8d and g). Regarding the XPS results, it could be implied that there were many components of C, N, and O groups (derived from both RWC-L/PAN precursor and TL-CD additive) existed on the CF surface. The presence of functional moieties could promote the wettability on carbonaceous surfaces and decrease the charge transfer resistance as well, resulting in the improvement of the electrochemical properties of carbon materials [52].

The ultimate analysis of as-prepared CFs was also performed using a combustion CHN analyzer to determine the total elemental CHNO composition of the whole sample (not just at the sample surface) as presented in Table 2 . The N and O atoms of unfilled CF sample were mainly derived from PAN (N and O) and RWC-L (O) precursors [16]. With the increment of CD concentrations from 0 to 0.6%w/v, the C and N elements tended to increase from 74.80 to 77.42 and 2.10 to 3.83 wt%, respectively, while the H and O elements declined from 2.30 to 1.00 and 20.80 to 17.52 wt%, respectively. After doping with as-synthesized CDs, more C and N atoms were received because CDs mainly consisted of C composition with highly N-containing functional groups. The C content of 0.4% w/v CD filled CFs increased from 72.03 to 79.68 with increasing carbonization temperature from 900 to 1400 °C. At high temperature, the conversion of precursors to carbonaceous materials with high C content was efficiently obtained since volatile substances as well as heteroatoms were feasibly eliminated [53]. Noted that the 0.4CD-C1200 had the highest N content of 4.74 wt%, which was expected to effectively enhance the surface wettability and electrical conductivity of the electrode material.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Crystalline structure, graphitic structure and electrical conductivity
ce:section.ce:para: The crystalline structure of carbonaceous materials can be qualitatively and quantitatively investigated by XRD analysis. As shown in Fig. 9a , two wide peaks at 2Ө ≅ 25.6 and 45° appeared on XRD patterns, indicating the (002) and (101) planes of graphitic layers, respectively, along with disordered microstructure [54]. The (002) peak typically involves the crystal size of graphite stacking layers with random orientation (turbostratic form). The (101) peak associates with the length between each carbon atom on the same plane. The peak intensity at 45° of CFs increased gradually as a function of increasing carbonization temperature, suggesting larger crystal dimension with the increment of carbonization temperature. This finding was in good agreement with the prior work [55].

Besides the surface area, pore structure, and functionality, the electrical conductivity is also a key parameter, affecting the electrochemical properties of electrode materials [9,10]. Typically, Raman spectroscopy results of carbon materials with graphitic structure compose of two intense-broad bands, which are G band (≅ 1550 cm−1) and D band (≅ 1340 cm−1) [56]. The G band refers to the sp2 carbons of the CC bond stretching, while the D band corresponds to the sp3 defects of carbon structure. The ratio of D band to G band intensities (ID/IG) can be used to evaluate disorders of carbon materials. Raman spectra with curve-fitting of prepared CFs are demonstrated in Fig. 9b, showing two characteristic bands of the d-D band at 1334–1343 cm−1 and the Gband at 1574–1594 cm−1. Table 3 presents the relationship between degree of structural disorder (ID/IG ratio) and electrical conductivity of as-prepared CFs. The ID/IG ratios of CFs containing various CD contents at same carbonization temperature were insignificantly different (≅ 0.96–0.98), which were higher than that of the 0.0CD-C1000 (0.93). Therefore, the CD filled CFs had a greater structural-disorder degree of graphitic carbon than that of the unfilled CFs. The results suggested that the sp2-carbon lattice might be defected from oxidation as well as hetero-groups on CF surfaces [57]. The 0.2CD-C1000 had lower electrical conductivity than that of the 0.0CD-C1000 owing to more defects and/ or large pores on the fiber structure, leading to less conductive areas [58]. However, the effect of nano-dimensional CDs was dominant at higher CD loadings, e.g., 0.4CD-C1000 and 0.6CD-C1000 samples, leading to the enhancement of electrical conductivities. The carbonization temperature (900–1400 °C) seemed to apparently affect the ID/IG ratio of CFs than the CD content, which were ≅ 0.97–1.12 for 0.4%w/v CD doped CFs. The electrical property of CFs carbonized at different temperatures directly related to the ID/IG ratio. As the ID/IG ratio increased, the electrical conductivity decreased. The 0.4CD-C1200 offered the highest electrical conductivity (≅ 62 S cm−1) with the lowest ID/IG ratio (≅ 0.97) because more graphitic structure was created together with higher C content. Conversely, the 0.4CD-C1400 exhibited the highest ID/IG ratio (≅ 1.12) with the lowest electrical conductivity (≅ 10 S cm−1), since the pore structure of CFs might be damaged with some defects and voids, resulting in more insulative regions on the fiber structure [58].

The CV measurement of as-prepared CF electrodes was performed in a three-electrode system using 30 mL of 6 M KOH electrolyte. Normally, KOH electrolyte is selected for well-developed microporous carbon-electrodes in electrochemical measurement. This was because of its excellent ionic conductivity, small ion dimension, and low corrosion, leading to great electron transfer, easy ion diffusion between electrode and electrolyte, and long electrode cycle-life [53,59]. Fig. 10a -d demonstrate the CV profiles of RWC-L/PAN based CF electrodes with and without CD doping at different scan rates. In the potential window of −1.2 to 0.1 V, the CV curve of all samples exhibited almost rectangular shapes, when the scan rate was decreased from 100 to 5 mV s−1, indicating more distinct EDLC behavior. The area of CV curves became larger with increasing scan rate, according to the enhancement of charging current density. The current density range of CV curves slightly changed as the concentration of CDs was increased at same scan rate. It is noticeable that the CV curve of various CD filled CF electrodes showed a larger current density than that of the unfilled CF electrode at a scan rate of 5 mV s−1 (Fig. 10e), suggesting the improvement of charge storage capability with the existence of CDs. This result was attributed to the surface functionalities and graphitic structure of CDs, leading to excellent surface wettability, short ionic-diffusion paths, and low ionic-diffusion resistance.

Fig. 11a -c depict the CV curves of 0.4CD-C900, 0.4CD-C1200, and 0.4CD-C1400 electrodes at different scan rates in 6 M KOH solution using a potential range of −1.2 to 0.1 V. As expected, the CV curve areas of all CF electrodes increased with increasing scan rate. Also, the nearly rectangular-like loop was obtained at low sweep rate. The 0.4CD-C1200 electrode provided the most rectangular-like shape along with the largest CV curve area, compared to those of CF electrodes carbonized at other temperatures (Fig. 11d). This suggested that an optimum charge storage performance was obtained at carbonization temperature of 1200 °C.

Based on the obtained CV curves, the estimated Cs of prepared CF electrodes at different scan rates are summarized in Table 4 . As the scan rate increased, the Cs of CF electrodes decreased, because of the limitation of charge transportation at high sweep rate [32]. At the same carbonization temperature, the Cs of all CD doped CF electrodes was distinctly larger than that of the undoped CF electrode. Due to the nano-dimension and hydrophilicity of CDs, the electrical conductivity and surface wettability of CF electrodes were significantly improved, leading to the enhancement of electroactive regions and effective ion transportation. However, the Cs was insignificantly increased with enhancing CD loading and had a trend to decrease at high CD content, e.g., 0.6CD-C1000 electrode. This might result from some parts of CD agglomeration (see Fig. 6a as an examples), leading to poor conductive networks on the fiber structure. It was reported that the well-dispersed CDs on the carbonaceous matrix significantly improved the effective surface area by generating a number of functionalized CD locations, thus enhancing capacitive performances [33]. The 0.4CD-C1000 electrode provided the highest Cs of ≅ 127 F g−1, while the 0.0CD-C1000 electrode gave the lowest Cs of ≅ 93 F g−1 at 5 mV s−1. The Cs of 0.2CD-C1000 (123 F g−1) and 0.6CD-C1000 (120 F g−1) electrodes were also larger than that of the 0.0CD-C1000 electrode at 5 mV s−1. This revealed that CDs played an importance role on enhancing Cs of CF electrodes, principally based on the synergy of EDLC and pseudo-capacitance effects. The latter was generated by heteroatoms from both CDs and RWC-L/PAN hybrid precursors [16,33].

At different carbonization temperatures, the calculated Cs from CV curves value was obviously improved, suggesting that the optimization of carbonization temperatures was an alternative way to enhance the capacitive performance of CD doped CF electrode materials. The 0.4CD-C1200 electrode gave the highest Cs of about 141 F g−1 at 5 mV s−1. This electrode possessed the highest SBET, Vtotal, and Vmicro together with sufficient functionalities and electrical conductivity, which were contributed to the most efficiency for ion transfer between electrode and electrolyte [53].

From the viewpoint of practical use, the Ragone plot of PDs versus EDs at various current densities in 6 M KOH electrolyte of the 0.4CD-C1200 electrode (Fig. 13a ) demonstrates that the electrochemical performance of this electrode was high enough for supercapacitor application. Fig. 13b compares the Nyquist plots between 0.0CD-C1000 and 0.4CD-C1200 electrodes. In the high frequency region, the intercept on the real axis (Z′ axis) typically indicates the total ohmic resistance (Rtotal), including the resistance of electrolyte and electrode as well as the contact resistance between them [60, 61]. The Rtotal value of the 0.4CD-C1200 electrode
( ≅
1.60 Ω) was lower than that of the other ( ≅ 1.64 Ω) as shown in Fig. 13b (inset). Moreover, the plot exhibited a semicircle-like curve at a high to medium frequency range, involving the resistance between electrode and electrolyte during charge transfer process. The smaller diameter the semicircle curve, the lower the interfacial resistance at the electrode-electrolyte interface [60]. Additionally, the shorter and sharper vertical line of Warburg impedance (W) in the low frequency region suggests better ion diffusion from electrolyte to the porous electrode, leading to the improvement of capacitive properties [61]. The 0.4CD-C1200 electrode presented smaller diameter of semicircle curve as well as the shorter and shaper W sloping line than that of the 0.0CD-C1000 electrode. Hence, the former had better EDLC behavior than the latter. Overall, the Nyquist plot confirmed the significant contribution of CD concentration and carbonization temperature to the electronic conductivity of electrode materials, corresponding to the Raman spectroscopy and electrical conductivity results.

The cyclability of the 0.4CD-C1200 electrode was evaluated at a current density of 5 A g−1 in 6 M KOH electrolyte as illustrated in Fig. 13c. The Cs slightly decreased after 12,000 cycles with the capacitance retention (CR) as high as
≅
90%, indicating excellent electrochemical stability. Moreover, the GCD curve of the 12000th cycle showed a lower charge-discharge cycle time with a larger IRdrop in comparison with those of the first cycle (Fig. 13c (inset)), resulting in the reduction of CR.

Table 6 summarizes the electrochemical-performance comparison between this work and mostly relevant works. According to the literature survey, the electrochemical properties of our work, especially for the cycling stability, were relatively superior to those of lignin/polymer-based CF electrodes with and without the addition of conductive additives. In addition, lignin/polymer CFs in the previous works were mainly doped with metal oxides, which needed multi-step preparation and/ or toxic chemicals to convert metal complexes to metal oxides [62, 63]. Hence, the utilization of a hybrid doping approach (bio-based CD filled RWC-L/PAN composite fibers) without any post treatment and binder composition could be an efficient way to produce green, renewable, and sustainable CF electrodes with high capacitive performances for next generation of energy storage devices.

The highly microporous CF fabrics with heteroatoms (N and O) were successfully fabricated from a hybrid polymer precursor of RWC-L and PAN via electrospinning and thermal treatment process. The superior electrochemical performance of as-prepared CF electrodes was achieved using a hybrid-doping approach, including the ex-situ doping by the TL-CD additive during fiber formation and the in-situ doping by the RWC-L/PAN precursor in carbonization process. The addition of functionalized CDs provided the improvement of electrode surface wettability as well as electroactive sites, thus, decreasing ionic diffusion paths and electronic resistance. Furthermore, the appropriate carbonization temperature played a significant role on not only the formation of low-defect graphitic carbon but also the creation of microporous structure with a large specific surface area. Combining these two factors, we found that the 0.4CD-C1200 electrode gave the maximum Cs (≅ 225 F g−1), ED (≅ 53 W h kg−1), and PD (≅ 130 W kg−1) at a current density of 0.2 A g−1 along with excellent cyclability up to 90% CR after 12,000 cycles at a current density of 5 A g−1. This was due to the synergetic effect of inherent EDLC and pseudo-capacitance. The obtained results demonstrate an efficient route to enhance capacitive properties of CF electrodes by decorating the CF matrix with optimized-pore structure and hydrophilic CDs. Hence, the CD/RWC-L/PAN CF material in the mat form exhibits a great potential for use as a binder-free electrode for high performance supercapacitors. Moreover, we also anticipate that the pore characteristic, surface area, and electrochemical properties of CD doped biomass-lignin/polymer CF sheets could be further improved by physical activation, e.g., CO2 activation during carbonization. This proposed method has been extensively and generally utilized in the field of carbon material synthesis for energy storage application, owing to its greatly practical and effective technology for mass-production in the future.
